did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) implemented with Keras and TensorFlow to perform multiclass classification of skin cancer lesions (melanoma, benign keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, etc.) using the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) framework for multiclass skin cancer classification using the HAM10000 dataset. It includes specific sections on methodology (Proposed Framework, System Evaluation), results (Confusion Matrix, Classification Report, Accuracy metrics), and a conclusion, which are characteristic of a primary research study rather than a review.

title: Multiclass Classification of Skin Cancer using Convolutional Neural Network
authors: S G Rao Bahadur Y. Mahabaleswrappa
journal: 2022 IEEE 2nd Mysore Sub Section International Conference (MysuruCon)
brief_summary: This study developed a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) framework using Keras and TensorFlow to perform multiclass classification of skin cancer lesions. The model was trained and evaluated on the publicly available HAM10000 dataset, which contains 10,015 dermatoscopic images across seven distinct skin lesion classes. To address class imbalance and prevent overfitting, the researchers employed data augmentation techniques and resized images to 100x125 pixels. The system's performance was assessed using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score metrics on a held-out test set. The proposed CNN achieved a testing accuracy of 77.91%, with macro and weighted average precision scores of 62.0 and 77.0, respectively. These results demonstrate the model's capability to effectively classify skin lesions and outperform several referenced state-of-the-art methods.

all_task_types.target: Seven distinct skin lesion classes (nv, mel, bkl, bcc, akiec, vasc, df)
all_task_types.details: Multiclass classification of skin cancer lesions using a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) on the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories for seven classes: Melanocytic Nevi (nv), Melanoma (mel), Benign Keratosis (bkl), Basal Cell Carcinoma (bcc), Actinic Keratosis (akiec), Vascular Lesions (vasc), and Dermatofibroma (df). Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset of dermatoscopic images obtained from Kaggle (originating from Harvard Dataverse). It contains 10,015 images across seven distinct skin lesion classes. The dataset was split into two parts for acquisition: HAM10000_images_part1 (5000 files) and HAM10000_images_part2 (5015 files).

use.training: Used for training the Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model. The training subset (6696 images) was subjected to data augmentation (zoom range, rotation range, horizontal/vertical flip) to address class imbalance and prevent overfitting.
use.validation: Used for validation during the model development process (744 images).
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of the model's performance, including calculation of accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score (2481 images).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score metrics on a held-out test set.

HAM10000.notes: The model achieved a testing accuracy of 77.91%. The macro average precision was 62.0 and weighted average precision was 77.0. The confusion matrix indicates that the 'bcc' class had the lowest recall (0.10) and F1-score (0.18), while 'akiec' had the highest precision (0.88) and recall (0.90). The paper states that performance parameters for all skin lesions were more than or equal to 0.40 score.

architecture_details.regularization: Dropout layers after each convolutional block
architecture_details.flattening: Flatten layer converting 3D feature maps to 1D vector

dense_layers.activation: Not explicitly stated, typically ReLU in Keras Dense

conclusions: The proposed Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) framework integrated with data augmentation successfully classified seven distinct skin lesion classes (nv, mel, bkl, bcc, akiec, vasc, df) using the HAM10000 dataset.
The model achieved a testing accuracy of 77.91% and a training accuracy of 86.54%.
The model obtained a macro average precision of 62.0 and a weighted average precision of 77.0.
The performance metrics (precision, recall, F1-score) for all seven skin lesion classes were found to be greater than or equal to 0.40.

The results of the proposed model were reported as superior to several state-of-the-art methods evaluated in the study, including DermofitImageTractCNN (75.1%), CNN-PA (72.1%), and Ensemble of Deep Neural Network (76.00%).
The study acknowledges that the model's performance is influenced by intra and inter-class diversity and similarities, which lead to misclassified samples.
Future work is identified as studying the employability, adaptability, and generality of the proposed pipeline architecture in other domains of disease classification.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a single public dataset (HAM10000) without external validation on independent cohorts, limiting generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.

The model exhibits significant class imbalance issues, evidenced by a very low recall (0.10) and F1-score (0.18) for Basal Cell Carcinoma (bcc), indicating poor sensitivity for this critical cancer type.
Performance metrics for Melanoma (mel) are modest (Precision: 0.58, Recall: 0.59, F1: 0.58), which is concerning given the high stakes of melanoma detection.
The paper does not report key training hyperparameters such as optimizer, loss function, number of epochs, or batch size, hindering reproducibility.

The comparison with state-of-the-art methods is flawed due to inconsistent dataset definitions; the proposed model uses 7 classes on HAM10000, while comparative models like DermofitImageTractCNN used 10 classes and others used different datasets (e.g., ISIC-2018, ISBI-2017).

Data leakage risk exists as the HAM10000 dataset contains multiple images from the same patients; the paper does not specify if patient-level splitting was used to prevent data leakage between training and test sets.
The input image resolution was significantly reduced from 600x450 to 100x125 pixels, which may result in loss of fine-grained diagnostic features necessary for accurate skin lesion classification.
The study lacks statistical significance testing (p-values, confidence intervals) to validate the superiority of the proposed model over comparative methods.

The macro average precision (0.62) is substantially lower than the weighted average precision (0.77), highlighting that the model performs poorly on minority classes despite good performance on majority classes.
